attributes.stem:
A 71-year-old man presents with a three-hour history of severe lumbar back pain. He feels light-headed and the pain is radiating into the loin. He had an episode of back pain without radiation to the loin one month ago.

On arrival at the Emergency Department, his pulse is 80/min and blood pressure is 142/80mmHg and there is widespread abdominal tenderness. An intravenous line is inserted and blood samples sent off for laboratory analysis. Thirty minutes later his pulse rate is 110/min, his blood pressure is 85/50mmHg and his abdominal tenderness is more evident. His femoral artery pulses cannot be felt.
attributes.stemQuestion: Apart from intravenous fluid replacement, which one of the following is the most appropriate next step in management?
attributes.explanation:
This man has a leaking abdominal aortic aneurysm. A CT scan may be appropriate in a haemodynamically stable patient, however, in this patient, the likely time delay may prove fatal. Whilst there is a role for endovascular aneurysm repair (EVAR), most clinicians prefer to undertake this procedure in a patient who is haemodynamically stable - and that is not the case here, therefore laparotomy with immediate control of the haemorrhage is the most appropriate management.

Abdominal aortic aneurysm (AAA) is an abnormal dilation of the abdominal aorta and represents a significant potential for morbidity and mortality. Risk factors for AAA include smoking, male sex, advanced age, atherosclerosis, connective tissue disease or further history of aneurysms. Risk factors for a ruptured AAA include a large initial diameter (>5.5cm), current smoking, hypertension, rapid expansion (>1.0 cm/year), female sex or symptomatic AAA.

AAA may be asymptomatic in many cases, and when AAA is symptomatic, it is a sign of rupture. Patients with a ruptured AAA may initially present with no symptoms, but most (75%) will present with pain, which is typically located in the abdomen and radiating to the groin but may also be in the back, flank or pelvis. Signs of shock may also be present with the classic triad of pain, pulsatile abdominal mass and hypotension present in 50% of patients with ruptured AAA.

Diagnosis of AAA depends on haemodynamic status. In unstable patients with a suspected ruptured AAA imaging is desirable but not essential prior to management. Bedside ultrasound may be used in this scenario to identify the AAA. In stable patients, urgent abdominal CT with contrast is preferable for diagnosis.

Management of ruptured AAA involves emergency and immediate control of haemorrhage in theatre with laparotomy followed by repair of the aneurysm.
learningPoints: Leaking aneurysms require rapid resuscitation and intervention.
references: Azhar B, Patel SR, Holt PJ, Hinchliffe RJ, Thompson MM, Karthikesalingam A. Misdiagnosis of ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysm: systematic review and meta-analysis. J Endovasc Ther. 2014;21(4):568-75.
Jeanmonod D, Yelamanchili VS, Jeanmonod R. Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm Rupture.  StatPearls. Treasure Island (FL)2020.

Rinckenbach S, Albertini JN, Thaveau F, Steinmetz E, Camin A, Ohanessian L, et al. Prehospital treatment of infrarenal ruptured abdominal aortic aneurysms: a multicentric analysis. Ann Vasc Surg. 2010;24(3):308-14.

Wanhainen A, Verzini F, Van Herzeele I, et al. European Society for Vascular Surgery (ESVS) 2019 Clinical Practice Guidelines on the Management of Abdominal Aorto-iliac Artery Aneurysms. Eur J Endovasc Surg 2019; 578-93.
